title: Potential Open Redirect Parameters
description: The page contains URL parameters or JavaScript patterns commonly associated with open redirect vulnerabilities.
evidence: Found redirect-related patterns in page source.
riskImpact: Open redirects can be used in phishing attacks by making malicious URLs appear to originate from your trusted domain.
explanation: Open redirect vulnerabilities occur when a web application takes a user-supplied URL parameter and redirects to it without validation.
fixSteps: Validate all redirect URLs against an allowlist.
Use relative URLs for redirects.
Reject any redirect URL that points to an external domain.
Implement server-side URL validation.

codeExamples.label: Validation

title: Email Address Exposure
description: Email addresses were found in the page source.
evidence: Found email addresses in page source.
riskImpact: Exposed email addresses can be harvested by bots for spam or targeted phishing.
explanation: Bots constantly crawl websites looking for email addresses in the HTML source.
fixSteps: Replace plaintext emails with contact forms.
Use JavaScript to dynamically render email addresses.
Encode email addresses using HTML entities.
Consider Cloudflare email obfuscation.

codeExamples.label: React Obfuscation

title: Directory Listing Appears Enabled
description: The response contains patterns indicating directory listing is enabled.
riskImpact: Attackers can browse your server's file structure, discovering sensitive files.
explanation: Directory listing allows anyone to see all files in a directory when no index file is present.
fixSteps: Disable directory listing in your web server configuration.
Ensure every directory has an index file.
Review exposed directories for sensitive files.

codeExamples.label: Nginx
codeExamples.code: autoindex off;

codeExamples.label: Apache
codeExamples.code: Options -Indexes

title: Sensitive File References Detected
description: The page references files commonly associated with sensitive configuration or development artifacts.
riskImpact: References to sensitive files may indicate they are accessible.
explanation: Sensitive files like .env, .git/, and configuration files should never be referenced in public pages.
fixSteps: Remove all references to sensitive files from public HTML.
Block access in your web server configuration.
Ensure .env, .git, and other config files are in .gitignore.

codeExamples.label: Nginx
codeExamples.code:
location ~ /\. { deny all; return 404; }
location ~ \.(env|sql|bak|config|log)$ { deny all; return 404; }

title: Potentially Outdated JavaScript Libraries
description: The page references JavaScript libraries with known security vulnerabilities.
evidence: Detected outdated libraries with known CVEs.
riskImpact: Outdated libraries contain publicly known vulnerabilities that attackers can exploit.
explanation: Using outdated client-side libraries exposes your application to known attack vectors.
fixSteps: Update all JavaScript libraries to their latest stable versions.
Use npm audit or Snyk to track vulnerabilities.
Set up Dependabot or Renovate for automated updates.
Remove unused libraries.

codeExamples.label: npm audit
codeExamples.code:
npm audit
npm audit fix
npm audit fix --force

title: Sensitive Paths Exposed in Robots.txt
description: The robots.txt file reveals potentially sensitive directory paths.
evidence: Sensitive disallowed paths found in robots.txt.
riskImpact: Robots.txt effectively creates a map of sensitive areas for attackers to probe.
explanation: Robots.txt files are publicly accessible and listing sensitive paths tells attackers exactly where to look.
fixSteps: Remove sensitive paths from robots.txt.
Ensure all sensitive endpoints require proper authentication.
Use auth and authorization rather than path hiding.

codeExamples.label: Secure robots.txt
codeExamples.code:
User-agent: *
Disallow: /api/
Sitemap: https://yourdomain.com/sitemap.xml

title: CMS / Technology Fingerprinting
description: The site exposes CMS or technology stack details that aid attacker reconnaissance.
riskImpact: Knowing the CMS type and version allows attackers to search for specific exploits.
explanation: CMS fingerprinting reveals the underlying technology, version, and sometimes plugin information.
fixSteps: Remove or obscure the generator meta tag.
Hide version numbers from public-facing pages.
Keep your CMS and all plugins updated.
Remove default installation files.

codeExamples.label: WordPress
codeExamples.code:
remove_action('wp_head', 'wp_generator');
add_filter('the_generator', '__return_empty_string');

title: Missing security.txt File
description: No reference to a security.txt file was found.
riskImpact: Security researchers may not know how to responsibly report vulnerabilities.
explanation: The security.txt file (RFC 9116) provides a standardized way for security researchers to find contact information.
fixSteps: Create a /.well-known/security.txt file.
Include Contact, Preferred-Languages, Expires fields.
Sign the file with PGP if possible.

codeExamples.label: security.txt
codeExamples.code:
Contact: mailto:security@yourdomain.com
Expires: 2026-12-31T23:59:00.000Z
Preferred-Languages: en

title: Potentially Dangerous Inline JavaScript
description: The page contains inline JavaScript with potentially dangerous patterns like eval(), document.write().
evidence: Found inline script blocks with dangerous patterns.
riskImpact: Dangerous JavaScript patterns can be exploited via XSS.
explanation: Patterns like eval(), document.write(), and direct innerHTML assignment with user input are common XSS vectors.
fixSteps: Replace eval() with JSON.parse().
Use textContent instead of innerHTML.
Move inline scripts to external files.
Use DOMPurify to sanitize HTML.

codeExamples.label: Safe alternatives

title: Server version disclosed in response header
description: A response header carries a value that matches a version-like pattern (e.g. '1.0', '2.3.1').
evidence: First response header whose value matches the version pattern is reported.
riskImpact: Version strings in headers give attackers a target list of CVEs without further probing.
explanation: Headers like Server, X-Powered-By, X-AspNet-Version, NEL, and Report-To frequently leak exact upstream versions. Strip them at the edge or use generic placeholders.
fixSteps: Set server_tokens off; in nginx or strip the Server header at the reverse proxy.
Disable X-Powered-By by setting `poweredByHeader: false` in Next.js or removing the Express x-powered-by setting.
Genericize NEL and Report-To headers or remove the 'version' field.

codeExamples.label: Nginx
Next.js
codeExamples.code: const nextConfig = { poweredByHeader: false };

title: Sensitive Information in HTML Comments
description: HTML comments containing potentially sensitive information detected.
riskImpact: HTML comments are visible to anyone viewing page source.
explanation: Developers often leave TODO notes, debug information, or temporary credentials in HTML comments.
fixSteps: Remove all HTML comments containing sensitive information.
Use a build process that strips comments.
Move debug notes to internal documentation.

codeExamples.label: HTML Minification
codeExamples.code:
const htmlnano = require('htmlnano');
htmlnano.process(html, { removeComments: 'all' });

title: Internal/Private IP Addresses Exposed
description: Internal or private IP addresses found in the page source.
riskImpact: Internal IP addresses reveal your network topology.
explanation: Private IP addresses and localhost references in public-facing pages leak information about your internal network structure.
fixSteps: Remove hardcoded internal IP addresses.
Use environment-specific configuration.
Review reverse proxy configurations.

codeExamples.label: Environment config

title: Debug Mode or Error Information Exposed
description: Debug/error indicators that reveal internal application details detected.
evidence: Debug headers, stack traces, or error messages found.
riskImpact: Debug output exposes internal file paths, framework versions, database types, and application logic.
explanation: Debug mode and verbose error messages are invaluable during development but catastrophic in production.
fixSteps: Disable debug mode in production.
Configure custom error pages.
Remove fingerprinting headers.
Set up error monitoring (Sentry).

codeExamples.label: Express.js
Next.js
codeExamples.code: const nextConfig = { poweredByHeader: false };

title: Insecure Iframe Sources on HTTPS Page
description: Iframes loading content over HTTP on an HTTPS page.
evidence: Found iframes using http:// src on HTTPS page.
riskImpact: HTTP iframes on HTTPS pages create mixed content, allowing MITM attackers to modify iframe content.
explanation: Browsers display your page as secure but an HTTP iframe can be intercepted and modified by network attackers.
fixSteps: Change all iframe src attributes to use HTTPS.
Use CSP: frame-src https:.
Consider using the sandbox attribute.

codeExamples.label: CSP
codeExamples.code: Content-Security-Policy: frame-src https: 'self';

title: Authentication Tokens Exposed in Page Source
description: Tokens or session identifiers exposed in the HTML source.
evidence: JWT tokens, Bearer tokens, or session IDs found in page source.
riskImpact: Exposed tokens allow session hijacking.
explanation: Authentication tokens should never appear in HTML source. They can be captured by browser extensions, cached by CDNs, or logged by proxies.
fixSteps: Store tokens in HTTP-only Secure cookies.
If tokens must be passed to JavaScript, inject them via a secure API call.
Never pass tokens in URL query parameters.

codeExamples.label: Secure cookie
codeExamples.code: response.cookies.set('session', token, { httpOnly: true, secure: true, sameSite: 'lax', maxAge: 86400, path: '/' });

title: Missing Autocomplete Attributes on Sensitive Fields
description: Sensitive input fields without proper autocomplete attributes.
evidence: Password or credit card fields missing autocomplete directives.
riskImpact: Browsers may cache sensitive form data on shared or compromised devices.
explanation: The autocomplete attribute tells browsers how to handle form autofill.
fixSteps: Add autocomplete="new-password" for registration fields.
Add autocomplete="current-password" for login fields.
Add appropriate autocomplete values for payment fields.

codeExamples.label: Proper autocomplete
codeExamples.code:
<input type="password" autocomplete="current-password" />
<input type="password" autocomplete="new-password" />

title: Forms Targeting New Windows
description: Forms with target="_blank" can be abused for phishing via reverse tabnabbing.
evidence: Form element(s) submit to a new window/tab.
riskImpact: Form submissions opening new windows can confuse users and be exploited.
explanation: Forms that open results in new tabs create the same reverse tabnabbing risk as anchor links.
fixSteps: Remove target="_blank" from forms unless necessary.
Consider handling form submission via fetch instead.

codeExamples.label: AJAX submission

title: Meta Refresh Redirect Detected
description: Meta refresh redirects can be abused for open redirect attacks and phishing.
evidence: Meta http-equiv="refresh" tag(s) with URL redirects.
riskImpact: Meta refresh redirects bypass standard navigation controls.
explanation: Meta refresh tags automatically redirect users after a delay. Unlike server-side redirects, they execute in the browser.
fixSteps: Replace meta refresh with server-side 301/302 redirects.
Never allow user-controlled input in meta refresh URLs.

codeExamples.label: Server redirect
codeExamples.code:
import { redirect } from 'next/navigation';
redirect('/new-page');

title: Insecure Base Tag Detected
description: The page uses a <base> tag with an HTTP URL, making all relative URLs resolve insecurely.
evidence: Found <base> tag(s) pointing to insecure HTTP origin.
riskImpact: All relative links, scripts, images resolve against the insecure base URL.
explanation: The HTML <base> tag sets the base URL for all relative URLs in the document.
fixSteps: Change the <base> tag to use HTTPS.
Remove the <base> tag if not needed.
Implement CSP base-uri directive.

codeExamples.label: CSP restriction
codeExamples.code: Content-Security-Policy: base-uri 'self'

title: postMessage Listener Without Origin Validation
description: postMessage event listeners don't appear to validate the message origin.
riskImpact: Any website can send messages to your page via postMessage.
explanation: If the receiving page doesn't verify event.origin, any malicious page can send crafted messages.
fixSteps: Always check event.origin against a whitelist.
Validate the structure and type of event.data.
Use event.source to verify the sender.

codeExamples.label: Safe listener

title: Sensitive Endpoints Referenced in Page Source
description: References to potentially sensitive endpoints in the page source.
riskImpact: References to admin panels, debug endpoints help attackers map your attack surface.
explanation: Even if endpoints are protected, exposing their paths gives attackers specific targets.
fixSteps: Remove references to internal/admin endpoints from client-side code.
Use server-side rendering for admin navigation.
Move API documentation behind authentication.

codeExamples.label: Conditional render
codeExamples.code: {session?.isAdmin && <Link href="/admin">Admin Panel</Link>}

title: Dangerous HTML Attributes Detected
description: Potentially dangerous HTML attributes like inline event handlers, javascript: URIs.
riskImpact: Inline event handlers and javascript: URIs are common XSS vectors.
explanation: Inline event handlers, javascript: protocol links, and data: URIs embedding HTML/JS are all classic XSS injection points.
fixSteps: Remove all javascript: protocol links.
Move inline event handlers to external JavaScript files.
Implement CSP with 'unsafe-inline' disabled.

codeExamples.label: Safe pattern
codeExamples.code: Click

title: JWT Token Exposed in URL
description: A JSON Web Token was found embedded in a URL within the page HTML.
riskImpact: The token appears in browser history, proxy logs, server logs, and Referer headers.
explanation: JWTs in URLs persist in numerous locations, creating many avenues for token theft.
fixSteps: Transmit tokens in HTTP headers (Authorization: Bearer) instead of URLs.
Use HTTP-only secure cookies.
Implement short-lived single-use tokens.

codeExamples.label: Fetch with header

title: Sensitive Information in Meta Tags
description: Meta tags that expose sensitive or unnecessary information.
riskImpact: Meta tag contents are visible in page source.
explanation: Generator tags reveal exact software versions with known CVEs. CSRF tokens in meta tags are accessible to any script.
fixSteps: Remove generator meta tags in production.
Move CSRF tokens to HTTP-only cookies.
Never store API keys in meta tags.

codeExamples.label: WordPress
codeExamples.code: remove_action('wp_head', 'wp_generator');

title: Sensitive Data Stored in Browser Storage APIs
description: JavaScript stores sensitive values (tokens, secrets) in localStorage or sessionStorage.
evidence: Sensitive data in browser storage APIs.
riskImpact: Any XSS vulnerability allows attackers to steal all data from browser storage.
explanation: Web Storage APIs are fully accessible to JavaScript. If an attacker exploits any XSS, they can read all stored values.
fixSteps: Move authentication tokens to HTTP-only, Secure, SameSite cookies.
Never store passwords, API keys, or PII in browser storage.
Implement CSP to reduce XSS risk.

codeExamples.label: Secure cookie instead
codeExamples.code:
// BAD: localStorage.setItem('token', jwt);
// GOOD: Set via server Set-Cookie: session=abc; HttpOnly; Secure; SameSite=Strict

title: CDN Resources Loaded Without Subresource Integrity
description: Scripts loaded from CDNs lack integrity attributes.
riskImpact: A compromised CDN could serve malicious scripts to all your users.
explanation: SRI lets you provide a cryptographic hash of the expected file content. The browser verifies before executing.
fixSteps: Add integrity and crossorigin attributes to all CDN resources.
Consider self-hosting critical libraries as an alternative.

codeExamples.label: SRI example
codeExamples.code: <script src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/bootstrap@5.3.0/dist/js/bootstrap.min.js" integrity="sha384-..." crossorigin="anonymous"></script>

title: Suspicious Content in Open Graph Tags
description: Open Graph meta tags contain suspicious content that could indicate injection.
riskImpact: Malicious OG content can be exploited by link preview services.
explanation: Some preview renderers process OG values in ways that can be exploited.
fixSteps: Sanitize all Open Graph tag content.
Validate og:url and og:image point to your own domains.
Never include user-controlled input in OG tags without encoding.

codeExamples.label: Safe OG tags
codeExamples.code:
<meta property="og:url" content="https://example.com/page" />
<meta property="og:image" content="https://example.com/image.jpg" />

title: Service Worker Registered Over Insecure HTTP
description: A service worker is being registered from an HTTP URL.
riskImpact: A MITM attacker could replace the service worker with malicious code that persists.
explanation: Service workers intercept all network requests. A compromised one gives attackers persistent control.
fixSteps: Serve the service worker file over HTTPS.
Use relative URLs for registration.

codeExamples.label: Safe registration
codeExamples.code:
// BAD: navigator.serviceWorker.register('http://example.com/sw.js');
// GOOD: navigator.serviceWorker.register('/sw.js');

title: Window.opener Access Detected
description: JavaScript accesses window.opener properties, exploitable for reverse tabnabbing.
riskImpact: Can redirect the parent tab to a phishing page without the user noticing.
explanation: When a page is opened via window.open() or target='_blank', it may have a reference to the opening window.
fixSteps: Add rel='noopener noreferrer' to external links.
Set window.opener = null at the top of pages.
Use Cross-Origin-Opener-Policy: same-origin.

codeExamples.label: Safe link
codeExamples.code: External

title: Insecure WebSocket Connection (ws://)
description: WebSocket connections using unencrypted ws:// instead of wss://.
riskImpact: Unencrypted WebSocket connections can be intercepted. WebSocket connections don't enforce same-origin policy.
explanation: ws:// transmits data in plaintext. WebSocket connections aren't bound by same-origin policy, making them vulnerable to cross-site hijacking.
fixSteps: Use wss:// for all WebSocket connections.
Validate the Origin header on the server.
Implement CSRF token verification in the handshake.

codeExamples.label: Secure WebSocket
codeExamples.code:
// BAD: new WebSocket('ws://example.com/feed');
// GOOD: new WebSocket('wss://example.com/feed');

title: Deprecated document.domain Usage Detected
description: The page sets document.domain, a deprecated practice that relaxes same-origin policy.
riskImpact: Setting document.domain weakens same-origin isolation.
explanation: document.domain was historically used for cross-subdomain communication. Chrome has deprecated it. Use postMessage instead.
fixSteps: Replace document.domain with postMessage().
Use CORS headers for cross-origin API requests.
Consider Channel Messaging API.

codeExamples.label: Use postMessage

title: Weak or Broken Cryptography Detected
description: Weak or broken cryptographic algorithms in client-side JavaScript.
riskImpact: Weak cryptography provides a false sense of security. MD5/SHA-1 can be forged, DES/RC4 broken, Math.random() is predictable.
explanation: Cryptographic algorithms become weak as computing power increases. MD5 collisions can be generated in seconds.
fixSteps: Replace MD5/SHA-1 with SHA-256 or SHA-3.
Replace DES/RC4 with AES-256-GCM.
Use crypto.getRandomValues() instead of Math.random().
Use the SubtleCrypto API.

codeExamples.label: Web Crypto API

title: Password Fields Block Pasting
description: Password fields have paste functionality disabled, discouraging password manager use.
riskImpact: Users cannot paste from password managers, leading to weaker passwords.
explanation: Both NCSC (UK) and NIST guidelines explicitly recommend against disabling paste on password fields.
fixSteps: Remove all onpaste event handlers from password fields.
Allow paste on all form fields, especially password fields.

codeExamples.label: Allow paste
codeExamples.code:
<!-- BAD -->
<input type="password" onpaste="return false">
<!-- GOOD -->
<input type="password" autocomplete="current-password">

title: Application Error Messages Exposed to Users
description: Detailed error messages visible in the page HTML.
riskImpact: Detailed errors reveal file paths, database types, library versions.
explanation: Verbose error messages reveal the technology stack and internal structure.
fixSteps: Configure production to display generic error pages.
Log detailed errors server-side only.
Implement custom error handlers.

codeExamples.label: Next.js error handling

title: Server-Side XML Parsing Detected
description: Server-side XML parsing library usage found. Ensure external entity processing is disabled.
riskImpact: If external entities are not disabled, XXE attacks can read local files or perform SSRF.
explanation: Server-side XML parsers can be vulnerable to XXE if not properly configured.
fixSteps: Disable external entity processing.
Validate that noent: false and nonet: true are set.
Consider using JSON instead of XML.

codeExamples.label: Safe xml2js config
codeExamples.code: const parser = new xml2js.Parser({ strict: true, normalize: true, xmlns: false });

title: Potential Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) Vectors
description: User-controlled input may be used in server-side HTTP requests.
riskImpact: SSRF allows attackers to access internal resources, cloud metadata services, or internal networks.
explanation: SSRF occurs when an application fetches a remote resource without validating the user-supplied URL.
fixSteps: Validate URLs against an allowlist.
Block requests to private IP ranges.
Disable redirects or validate redirect destinations.

codeExamples.label: SSRF prevention

title: GraphQL Introspection Enabled in Production
description: GraphQL introspection is enabled, exposing the entire API schema.
evidence: Found GraphQL introspection queries or GraphiQL interface.
riskImpact: Attackers can discover all queries, mutations, types, and fields.
explanation: Introspection is useful for development but should be disabled in production.
fixSteps: Disable introspection in production.
Use environment variables to conditionally enable.
Implement authentication before allowing introspection.
Consider persisted queries.

codeExamples.label: Disable introspection
codeExamples.code: const server = new ApolloServer({ typeDefs, resolvers, introspection: process.env.NODE_ENV !== 'production' });

title: Iframes Missing Sandbox Attribute
description: Multiple iframes found without the sandbox attribute, allowing embedded content full capabilities.
riskImpact: Unsandboxed iframes have full access to browser features, forms, scripts, and popups.
explanation: The sandbox attribute restricts what an iframe can do. Without it, embedded content has nearly the same capabilities as the parent page.
fixSteps: Add the sandbox attribute to all iframes.
Only enable needed capabilities: allow-scripts, allow-same-origin, etc.

codeExamples.label: Sandboxed iframe
codeExamples.code: <iframe src="https://embed.example.com" sandbox="allow-scripts allow-same-origin" loading="lazy"></iframe>

title: Password Fields Missing Name/Autocomplete Attributes
description: Password input fields are missing name or autocomplete attributes, hindering password manager functionality.
evidence: Password field(s) missing name or autocomplete attributes.
riskImpact: Password managers may not correctly identify or fill these fields, discouraging their use.
explanation: Password managers rely on input name and autocomplete attributes to correctly identify and autofill fields.
fixSteps: Add name and autocomplete attributes to all password fields.
Use autocomplete='current-password' for login and 'new-password' for registration.

codeExamples.label: Proper password field
codeExamples.code: <input type="password" name="password" autocomplete="current-password" />

title: POST Forms Without CSRF Tokens
description: POST form(s) found without apparent CSRF token fields. Note: This check skips framework apps (Next.js, Nuxt) that handle CSRF differently.
evidence: POST form(s) without CSRF token fields.
riskImpact: Without CSRF tokens, attackers can trick users into submitting forms from malicious sites.
explanation: CSRF attacks forge requests from authenticated users. CSRF tokens ensure form submissions originate from your site.
fixSteps: Add a hidden CSRF token field to all POST forms.
Validate the token on the server for every form submission.
Use the SameSite cookie attribute as additional protection.

codeExamples.label: CSRF token
codeExamples.code:
<form method="POST" action="/submit">
  <input type="hidden" name="csrf_token" value="{{token}}" />
</form>

title: API Version Information Exposed
description: API version numbers or build identifiers are exposed in headers or page source.
riskImpact: Version numbers help attackers identify specific vulnerabilities for that version.
explanation: Exposing exact version numbers aids attacker reconnaissance. Remove or obfuscate version info in production.
fixSteps: Remove version headers like X-API-Version.
Don't include build IDs in client-facing code.
Use generic version references if needed.

codeExamples.label: Remove headers

title: Missing HTML lang Attribute
description: The <html> tag does not include a lang attribute, impacting accessibility and SEO.
evidence: The <html> tag does not include a lang attribute.
riskImpact: Screen readers may not announce content in the correct language. Search engines may misidentify the page language.
explanation: The lang attribute on <html> tells browsers and assistive technologies the primary language of the page.
fixSteps: Add lang attribute to the <html> tag: <html lang="en">.
Use appropriate BCP 47 language codes.

codeExamples.label: HTML
codeExamples.code: <html lang="en">

title: Forms Submitting to External Domains
description: Form(s) found that submit data to external third-party domains (excluding known payment providers).
riskImpact: Form data may be sent to untrusted external servers.
explanation: Forms with external action URLs send user data outside your domain. While legitimate for payment forms, unexpected external submissions may indicate compromise.
fixSteps: Review all external form actions for legitimacy.
Use server-side proxying for third-party form submissions.
Add CSP form-action to restrict allowed targets.

codeExamples.label: CSP form-action
codeExamples.code: Content-Security-Policy: form-action 'self' https://checkout.stripe.com;

title: Sensitive Data in Browser Storage
description: Sensitive data (tokens, passwords, API keys) being stored in localStorage or sessionStorage.
evidence: Sensitive data stored in browser storage.
riskImpact: Any XSS vulnerability allows complete theft of stored sensitive data.
explanation: Browser storage APIs have no access controls. Any script on the page can read all stored values.
fixSteps: Move tokens to HTTP-only cookies.
Never store passwords or API keys client-side.

codeExamples.label: Use cookies
codeExamples.code:
// Instead of localStorage, use HTTP-only cookies
response.cookies.set('session', token, { httpOnly: true, secure: true, sameSite: 'strict' });

title: Viewport Prevents User Zoom
description: The viewport meta tag disables user scaling (user-scalable=no or maximum-scale=1), which is an accessibility issue.
riskImpact: Users with visual impairments cannot zoom the page, violating WCAG accessibility guidelines.
explanation: Disabling zoom creates an accessibility barrier for users who need to enlarge content.
fixSteps: Remove user-scalable=no from viewport meta.
Remove or increase maximum-scale.
Allow natural pinch-to-zoom behavior.

codeExamples.label: Accessible viewport
codeExamples.code: <meta name="viewport" content="width=device-width, initial-scale=1">

title: Hardcoded IP Addresses in Page Source
description: Multiple distinct IP addresses found hardcoded in page source (excluding localhost and common non-sensitive IPs).
evidence: IP addresses found in page source.
riskImpact: Hardcoded IPs reveal server infrastructure and network topology.
explanation: IP addresses in client-facing code suggest hardcoded infrastructure references that should use DNS or environment variables.
fixSteps: Replace hardcoded IPs with domain names.
Use environment variables for server addresses.
Configure DNS properly.

codeExamples.label: Use DNS
codeExamples.code: const API = process.env.API_URL; // Not a hardcoded IP

title: document.write() Usage Detected
description: document.write()/document.writeln() usage found. These are deprecated and can be exploited for XSS.
riskImpact: document.write() can be hijacked to inject malicious content if any input is attacker-controlled.
explanation: document.write() is deprecated, blocks HTML parsing, and is a common XSS vector.
fixSteps: Replace document.write() with DOM APIs (createElement, appendChild).
Use innerHTML with DOMPurify if HTML insertion is needed.

codeExamples.label: Safe alternative

title: Excessive Third-Party Domain Connections
description: The page connects to a large number of distinct third-party domains (10+), increasing attack surface.
riskImpact: Each third-party domain is a potential attack vector. If any is compromised, it can affect your users.
explanation: Every external domain you load resources from expands your attack surface. A compromise of any CDN, analytics, or advertising domain can lead to script injection.
fixSteps: Audit all third-party connections and remove unnecessary ones.
Self-host critical resources where possible.
Use SRI for all external scripts.
Implement a strict CSP to limit allowed domains.

codeExamples.label: CSP allowlist
codeExamples.code: Content-Security-Policy: script-src 'self' https://trusted-cdn.com;

title: Text Inputs Missing maxlength Attribute
description: Multiple text inputs and textareas found without maxlength, allowing unbounded input.
riskImpact: Unbounded input fields can be used for denial-of-service or buffer-based attacks on backend systems.
explanation: Without maxlength, users (or attackers) can submit extremely long strings that may cause performance issues or exploit backend vulnerabilities.
fixSteps: Add maxlength attribute to all text inputs and textareas.
Validate input length on the server side as well.

codeExamples.label: HTML
codeExamples.code:
<input type="text" maxlength="255" />
<textarea maxlength="5000"></textarea>

title: Images Missing Lazy Loading
description: Many images found without loading='lazy' attribute, impacting page load performance.
riskImpact: All images load immediately, increasing initial page load time and bandwidth usage.
explanation: Native lazy loading defers offscreen images until the user scrolls near them, improving initial load performance.
fixSteps: Add loading='lazy' to images that are below the fold.
Keep loading='eager' for above-the-fold images.

codeExamples.label: Lazy loading

title: Cookie With Excessively Long Expiration
description: Cookie set to expire far in the future (over 1 year).
riskImpact: Long-lived cookies increase the window for session theft.
explanation: Cookies that persist for years are a security risk.
fixSteps: Set reasonable max-age values; use session cookies where possible.

codeExamples.label: Disable source maps in production

title: AWS Metadata Endpoint Reference
description: Reference to AWS metadata endpoint (169.254.169.254) detected.
riskImpact: SSRF to metadata endpoint can steal IAM credentials.
explanation: The AWS metadata service provides instance credentials.
fixSteps: Block metadata access from applications; use IMDSv2.

codeExamples.label: Block access to AWS metadata endpoint

title: .git Directory Paths Exposed
description: .git directory paths found in page source.
riskImpact: Exposed .git allows downloading entire source code history.
explanation: If .git is accessible, attackers can reconstruct your entire repository.
fixSteps: Block access to .git directory in web server config.

codeExamples.label: Block access to .git directory

title: .env File Reference Detected
description: References to .env file found in page source.
riskImpact: Exposed .env files contain database credentials, API keys, and secrets.
explanation: .env files should never be web-accessible.

codeExamples.label: Use POST for forms that handle sensitive data
codeExamples.code:

<!-- <form method="GET" action="/search-private"> -->

<!-- GOOD: POST for forms with sensitive data -->
<form method="POST" action="/api/submit">
  <input type="hidden" name="_csrf" value="{{ csrfToken }}" />
  <input type="password" name="password" autocomplete="current-password" />
  <button type="submit">Submit</button>
</form>

<!-- <meta name="referrer" content="unsafe-url"> -->

<!-- Or via HTTP header (preferred over meta):
     Referrer-Policy: strict-origin-when-cross-origin -->

<input type="password" name="current-password" autocomplete="current-password" />
<input type="password" name="new-password" autocomplete="new-password" />
<input type="password" name="confirm-password" autocomplete="new-password" />
<!-- autocomplete="off" on password fields is ignored by most modern browsers
     and prevents password manager use (bad for security) -->

title: Form Disables Autocomplete
description: Form has autocomplete=off.
riskImpact: Prevents password manager functionality.
explanation: Users may enter weaker manual passwords.
fixSteps: Allow autocomplete unless there's a specific reason.

codeExamples.label: Remove autocomplete="off" from forms
codeExamples.code:
<!-- autocomplete="off" prevents password manager autofill
     and is ignored by most modern browsers for passwords -->

<!-- BAD -->
<!-- <form autocomplete="off"> -->

<!-- GOOD: allow autocomplete at form level,
     set specific autocomplete values on each field -->
<form>
  <input type="email" name="email" autocomplete="email" />
  <input type="password" name="password" autocomplete="current-password" />
</form>

title: Password Field with Short maxlength
description: Password input with very short maxlength.
riskImpact: Artificially limits password strength.
explanation: Short passwords are easier to crack.
fixSteps: Allow at least 64-128 character passwords.

codeExamples.label: Set appropriate maxlength values on inputs
codeExamples.code:
<!-- NIST recommends allowing passwords up to 64+ characters -->
<input type="password" name="password" maxlength="128" autocomplete="current-password" />
<input type="email" name="email" maxlength="254" autocomplete="email" />

<!-- Set generous limits that do not frustrate users or break password managers -->
<!-- Validate length server-side as well -->

title: Hidden Password Input Field
description: Password field with type=hidden.
riskImpact: May expose password in page source.
explanation: Hidden inputs are visible in HTML source.
fixSteps: Never store passwords in hidden fields.

codeExamples.label: Avoid storing passwords in hidden form fields
codeExamples.code:
<!-- BAD: password in a hidden field is visible in source and form data -->
<!-- <input type="hidden" name="password" value="{{ password }}"> -->

<!-- GOOD: request the password again for re-authentication flows -->
<input type="password" name="password" autocomplete="current-password" />
<!-- Never pass passwords through hidden fields across multiple form steps -->

title: Password Field Type is Text
description: Password stored in text input instead of password type.
riskImpact: Password visible on screen.
explanation: Password fields should mask input.
fixSteps: Use type='password' for password inputs.

codeExamples.label: Default password fields to type="password" (hidden)

title: Sensitive Data in Readonly Field
description: Sensitive data in readonly input field.
riskImpact: Users can still copy, data in DOM.
explanation: Readonly doesn't prevent data access.
fixSteps: Consider masking or removing sensitive readonly data.

codeExamples.label: Do not make sensitive fields readonly as a security measure
codeExamples.code:
<!-- readonly only prevents editing in the UI — values are still submitted -->
<!-- Attackers can remove readonly via browser devtools and change the value -->

<!-- GOOD: validate all field values server-side regardless of readonly state -->
export async function POST(req: Request) {
  const { accountId } = await req.json();
  // Verify the user has permission for this accountId server-side
  const session = await getSession();
  if (session.userId !== accountId) {
    return Response.json({ error: "Forbidden" }, { status: 403 });
  }
}

title: File Upload Without Type Restrictions
description: File input without accept attribute.
riskImpact: Malicious file uploads possible.
explanation: Accept attribute provides client-side hint.
fixSteps: Set accept attribute and validate on server.

codeExamples.label: Validate file type, size, and content on upload

title: Multiple File Upload Enabled
description: File input allows multiple files.

explanation: Multiple files increases attack surface.

title: Source Map Files Exposed

explanation: Source maps help reverse-engineer code.

title: TODO/FIXME Comments Exposed

riskImpact: Reveals incomplete security work.
explanation: May indicate known issues.

codeExamples.label: Remove TODO/FIXME security comments before production
codeExamples.code:
# Find all security-related TODO/FIXME comments
grep -rn "TODO\|FIXME\|HACK\|XXX\|BUG\|SECURITY\|UNSAFE" src/ \
  --include="*.ts" --include="*.tsx" --include="*.js"

# Add ESLint rule to fail CI on security TODOs:
# eslint-plugin-no-warning-comments:

title: Iframe Missing Lazy Loading
description: Below-fold iframes without loading=lazy.
riskImpact: If exploited, this finding exposes the target to category-specific risks under content. Severity reflects the realistic worst-case impact under default configurations.
explanation: Lazy loading defers iframe loading.
fixSteps: Add loading='lazy' to non-critical iframes.

codeExamples.label: Add loading="lazy" to offscreen iframes
codeExamples.code:
<iframe
  src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/VIDEO_ID"
  loading="lazy"
  title="Product demo video"
  allow="autoplay; encrypted-media"
  sandbox="allow-scripts allow-same-origin"
></iframe>

title: Missing Preconnect for Third-Party
description: Third-party resources without preconnect hint.

riskImpact: Slower third-party resource loading.
explanation: Preconnect establishes early connections.
fixSteps: Add preconnect for critical third-party origins.

codeExamples.label: Add preconnect hints for critical third-party origins
codeExamples.code:
<!-- Add in <head> for origins that are critical to the render path -->
<link rel="preconnect" href="https://fonts.googleapis.com" />
<link rel="preconnect" href="https://fonts.gstatic.com" crossorigin />
<link rel="dns-prefetch" href="https://api.yourcdn.com" />

riskImpact: Slower DNS resolution.
explanation: DNS prefetch resolves domains early.
fixSteps: Add dns-prefetch for third-party domains.

<link rel="dns-prefetch" href="https://analytics.example.com" />
<link rel="dns-prefetch" href="https://fonts.googleapis.com" />

title: Meta refresh without URL

riskImpact: Use HTTP 301/302 instead of meta refresh
explanation: <meta http-equiv=refresh content='5'> without url= is informational; with url= it's a redirect.
fixSteps: Use HTTP 301/302 instead of meta refresh

codeExamples.label: Validate redirect parameters server-side

title: Iframe missing allowfullscreen attribute
description: Iframes without allowfullscreen can't fill the viewport on mobile.
evidence: Content-level finding for `content-iframe-missing-allowfullscreen` on the response body.
riskImpact: Add allowfullscreen attribute
explanation: Iframes without allowfullscreen can't fill the viewport on mobile.
fixSteps: Add allowfullscreen attribute

codeExamples.label: Add SRI hash to external stylesheets
codeExamples.code:
<!-- Generate: openssl dgst -sha384 -binary style.css | openssl base64 -A -->
<link
  rel="stylesheet"

  integrity="sha384-COMPUTED_HASH_HERE"

/>

title: Configuration file reference detected in body
description: The page references a configuration filename (config.yaml, config.json, .env, docker-compose) that often indicates server-side configuration files.
evidence: Configuration file reference found in body.
riskImpact: References to sensitive configuration may indicate they are exposed at the URL mentioned.
explanation: When a page surfaces the names of server-side configuration files, it provides a roadmap for an attacker probing for common misconfiguration paths.
fixSteps: Verify that the referenced paths (e.g. /config.yaml, /.env, /docker-compose.yml) return 404 on the public origin.
Block access to dotfiles and config files at the reverse proxy (location ~ /\. { deny all; }).
Remove in-content references unless they are user-facing documentation.
If these are documentation pages, ensure they are noindexed and not advertised in the sitemap.

title: Third-party iframe without sandbox
description: Third-party widgets (Stripe, YouTube) loaded without sandbox can run arbitrary scripts.
evidence: Content-level finding for `content-iframe-third-party-without-sandbox` on the response body.
riskImpact: Add sandbox='allow-scripts allow-same-origin allow-forms' to third-party iframes
explanation: Third-party widgets (Stripe, YouTube) loaded without sandbox can run arbitrary scripts.
fixSteps: Add sandbox='allow-scripts allow-same-origin allow-forms' to third-party iframes

codeExamples.label: Add sandbox to srcdoc iframes

title: Autofocus combined with positive tabindex
description: Combining autofocus with tabindex > 0 produces confusing focus order for keyboard and screen reader users.
evidence: Content-level finding for `content-autofocus-positive-tabindex` on the response body.
riskImpact: Remove positive tabindex from autofocused elements; let the natural tab order stand
explanation: Combining autofocus with tabindex > 0 produces confusing focus order for keyboard and screen reader users.
fixSteps: Remove positive tabindex from autofocused elements; let the natural tab order stand

codeExamples.label: Avoid positive tabindex values
codeExamples.code: Help

title: aria-hidden=true with focusable children
description: When aria-hidden=true is applied to a container that still contains focusable elements, keyboard users can tab into invisible controls.
evidence: Content-level finding for `content-aria-hidden-focusable-children` on the response body.
riskImpact: Add tabindex=-1 to focusable descendants, or remove aria-hidden from the parent
explanation: When aria-hidden=true is applied to a container that still contains focusable elements, keyboard users can tab into invisible controls.
fixSteps: Add tabindex=-1 to focusable descendants, or remove aria-hidden from the parent

codeExamples.label: Do not use aria-hidden on elements with focusable children

title: formnovalidate attribute on submit button
description: A submit button with formnovalidate bypasses HTML5 input constraints (required, pattern, type=email). Server-side validation must catch this.
evidence: Content-level finding for `content-form-formnovalidate-bypass` on the response body.
riskImpact: Always validate on the server; never rely on browser-side form validation alone
explanation: A submit button with formnovalidate bypasses HTML5 input constraints (required, pattern, type=email). Server-side validation must catch this.
fixSteps: Always validate on the server; never rely on browser-side form validation alone

codeExamples.label: Always validate server-side regardless of formnovalidate

title: Form action uses javascript: scheme
description: A <form action="javascript:..."> executes JavaScript on submit and is a classic XSS vector if the action is partially attacker-controlled.
evidence: Content-level finding for `content-form-action-javascript-scheme` on the response body.
riskImpact: Replace action="javascript:..." with action="/endpoint" and handle submit via JS or server
explanation: A <form action="javascript:..."> executes JavaScript on submit and is a classic XSS vector if the action is partially attacker-controlled.
fixSteps: Replace action="javascript:..." with action="/endpoint" and handle submit via JS or server

codeExamples.label: Never use javascript: in form action
codeExamples.code:
<!-- BAD: form action with javascript: scheme executes arbitrary JS -->
<!-- <form action="javascript:void(0)" onsubmit="handle()"> -->

<!-- GOOD: use a real action URL or submit via JavaScript -->
<form id="myForm" action="/api/submit" method="POST">
  <!-- ... -->
</form>
<script>
document.getElementById("myForm").addEventListener("submit", async (e) => {
  e.preventDefault();
  await fetch("/api/submit", { method: "POST", body: new FormData(e.target) });
});
</script>

title: Form action uses mailto: scheme
description: A <form action="mailto:..."> opens the user's mail client, leaks the recipient address, and is unreliable for data submission.
evidence: Content-level finding for `content-form-action-mailto-scheme` on the response body.
riskImpact: Use a server endpoint or contact form instead of mailto: for form submission
explanation: A <form action="mailto:..."> opens the user's mail client, leaks the recipient address, and is unreliable for data submission.
fixSteps: Use a server endpoint or contact form instead of mailto: for form submission

codeExamples.label: Replace mailto: form actions with a server-side form handler

title: Sandboxed iframe allows both scripts and same-origin
description: Combining sandbox='allow-scripts allow-same-origin' removes most of the sandbox protection and lets the iframe access the parent origin.
evidence: Content-level finding for `content-iframe-allow-scripts-allow-same-origin` on the response body.
riskImpact: Drop allow-same-origin (or allow-scripts) - together they fully re-enable same-origin script execution
explanation: Combining sandbox='allow-scripts allow-same-origin' removes most of the sandbox protection and lets the iframe access the parent origin.
fixSteps: Drop allow-same-origin (or allow-scripts) - together they fully re-enable same-origin script execution

codeExamples.label: Avoid combining allow-scripts and allow-same-origin in sandbox
codeExamples.code:
<!-- DANGEROUS: combining these two effectively removes sandbox protection -->
<!-- The iframe can remove its own sandbox via script -->
<!-- BAD: sandbox="allow-scripts allow-same-origin" for cross-origin content -->

<!-- GOOD: use allow-scripts only for cross-origin untrusted content -->
<iframe src="https://untrusted.example.com" sandbox="allow-scripts" title="Widget"></iframe>
<!-- Only combine allow-scripts + allow-same-origin for your own trusted, same-origin content -->

title: SVG with inline onload handler
description: An <svg onload="..."> attribute fires JavaScript when the SVG renders and is equivalent to inline script for XSS purposes.
evidence: Content-level finding for `content-svg-onload-handler` on the response body.
riskImpact: Move event handlers to addEventListener in external JS; sanitize SVG uploads
explanation: An <svg onload="..."> attribute fires JavaScript when the SVG renders and is equivalent to inline script for XSS purposes.
fixSteps: Move event handlers to addEventListener in external JS; sanitize SVG uploads

codeExamples.label: Strip onload and event handlers from SVG before rendering

title: SVG with external entity reference (XXE)
description: An <svg> with an external entity reference like <!ENTITY xxe SYSTEM "file:///..."> can leak local files when the SVG is rendered server-side.
evidence: Content-level finding for `content-svg-external-entity-reference` on the response body.
riskImpact: Strip DOCTYPE and external entities when ingesting SVG; use a hardened parser
explanation: An <svg> with an external entity reference like <!ENTITY xxe SYSTEM "file:///..."> can leak local files when the SVG is rendered server-side.
fixSteps: Strip DOCTYPE and external entities when ingesting SVG; use a hardened parser

codeExamples.label: Disable external entity references in SVG parsing

title: Missing sandbox attribute on iframe
description: An iframe element is present without a sandbox attribute, giving the embedded content full access to the same privileges as the parent page.
evidence: iframe without sandbox attribute detected at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Without sandbox, an iframe has the same-origin privileges as the parent page — third-party content can access parent DOM, cookies, and run scripts with no restrictions.
explanation: The sandbox attribute restricts iframe capabilities by default and allows adding back only the required permissions.
fixSteps: Add sandbox="" to the iframe element
Add specific permissions as needed: allow-scripts, allow-forms, allow-same-origin
Never combine allow-scripts with allow-same-origin for cross-origin untrusted content

codeExamples.label: Add sandbox attribute
codeExamples.code:
<!-- Unsafe: no sandbox -->
<iframe src="https://third-party.com"></iframe>

<!-- Safe: sandboxed -->
<iframe src="https://third-party.com" sandbox="allow-scripts allow-forms"></iframe>

title: Form action points to an insecure or external URL
description: A form action attribute uses HTTP or points to an external domain, risking credential submission to an untrusted destination.
evidence: Form with insecure or external action URL detected at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Forms that submit to HTTP URLs expose credentials in transit. Forms targeting external domains may submit user data to attacker-controlled servers — a common phishing vector.
explanation: Form actions should always point to HTTPS endpoints on your own domain.
fixSteps: Change all form actions to HTTPS URLs
Ensure the action domain is your own, not a third-party or user-controlled value
Validate the action URL server-side if it is dynamically generated

codeExamples.label: Restrict form action to known paths
codeExamples.code:
<!-- Unsafe: accepts any action value from user input -->
<form action="<?= $_GET['redirect'] ?>">...</form>

<!-- Safe: hardcoded action -->
<form action="/api/v1/login" method="post">...</form>

title: Autocomplete enabled on sensitive input fields
description: Sensitive form fields such as credit card numbers or security codes have browser autocomplete enabled, which may store sensitive data in the browser.
evidence: Sensitive input field with autocomplete enabled detected at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Browser-stored autocomplete data can be retrieved by malicious same-origin scripts via the Credential Management API, or exposed on shared/compromised devices.
explanation: Use specific autocomplete values for each field type; disable only where browser-stored values would be a security risk.
fixSteps: Set autocomplete="off" on fields like CVV/CVC that should never be stored
Use autocomplete="cc-number" for card number (allows cards but signals sensitive)
Use autocomplete="new-password" to prevent storing new passwords

codeExamples.label: Disable autocomplete on sensitive inputs
codeExamples.code:
<!-- Disable autocomplete on sensitive fields -->
<input type="password" autocomplete="new-password">
<input type="text" name="cvv" autocomplete="off">
<input type="text" name="otp" autocomplete="one-time-code">

title: Base tag with insecure or dynamic href
description: The HTML base element sets the base URL for all relative links. A user-controlled or HTTP base tag enables base tag hijacking attacks.
evidence: HTML base tag with insecure href detected at {{url}}.
riskImpact: An attacker who can inject a base tag pointing to their server can redirect all relative resource requests — loading attacker-controlled scripts, images, and forms while keeping the original domain in the address bar.
explanation: The base tag should be set to a fixed HTTPS absolute URL and never derived from user input.
fixSteps: Set base href to an absolute HTTPS URL you control
Never derive the base href from query parameters or cookies
Validate base tag values as part of your template security review

codeExamples.label: Remove or restrict base tag
codeExamples.code: Link

title: Missing preconnect hints for critical third-party origins
description: Third-party origins used for critical resources (fonts, CDN scripts) do not have preconnect link hints, delaying their connection setup.
evidence: Critical third-party connections without preconnect hints detected at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Without preconnect hints, DNS lookup, TCP handshake, and TLS negotiation for third-party origins happen when the browser encounters the resource — delaying render and interactivity (poor Core Web Vitals).
explanation: Preconnect hints tell the browser to establish connections to origins early, reducing latency for critical third-party resources.
fixSteps: Add <link rel="preconnect"> for origins used for critical render-path resources
Add crossorigin attribute when the resource uses CORS (e.g., fonts from Google Fonts)
Use dns-prefetch as a lighter-weight alternative for non-critical origins

codeExamples.label: Add rel=noopener to third-party preconnect
codeExamples.code:
<!-- Without referrer policy, preconnect leaks the origin -->
<link rel="preconnect" href="https://cdn.example.com">

<!-- With referrer policy -->
<link rel="preconnect" href="https://cdn.example.com" crossorigin>

title: Source maps exposed publicly in production
description: JavaScript source map (.map) files are publicly accessible, exposing original source code to anyone who downloads them.
evidence: JavaScript source map file publicly accessible at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Source maps reconstruct original TypeScript/JSX source code from minified bundles — attackers can use them to understand application logic, find hardcoded values, identify vulnerabilities, and make targeted attacks more efficient.
explanation: Source maps should be uploaded to error tracking services but not served publicly via the web server.
fixSteps: Disable public source map generation in your build config
Upload source maps to Sentry or your error tracker privately
Configure your web server to return 404 for *.map requests

codeExamples.label: Disable source maps in production (webpack)

title: WordPress login page publicly accessible
description: The WordPress admin login page (/wp-login.php or /wp-admin/) is publicly accessible, exposing it to brute-force attacks.
riskImpact: Publicly accessible WordPress login pages are continuously attacked by automated bots attempting credential stuffing and brute-force attacks. A weak admin password will be compromised quickly.
explanation: While hiding the login URL provides only obscurity, combining it with rate limiting, 2FA, and IP allowlisting significantly reduces attack surface.
fixSteps: Enable login rate limiting and CAPTCHA
Require two-factor authentication for all admin accounts
Restrict /wp-admin/ access to specific IP ranges at the server level
Keep WordPress and all plugins updated

codeExamples.label: Block wp-login.php in nginx

title: WebSocket connection uses unencrypted ws:// protocol
description: The page opens a WebSocket connection using the unencrypted ws:// protocol instead of the secure wss:// protocol.
riskImpact: An unencrypted WebSocket connection can be intercepted and read by any network observer or man-in-the-middle attacker — exposing all messages including authentication tokens, user data, and session information sent over the connection.
explanation: WebSocket connections should always use wss:// (WebSocket over TLS) in the same way that HTTP should be served over HTTPS.
fixSteps: Change all ws:// WebSocket URLs to wss://
Derive the protocol from the page protocol: location.protocol === "https:" ? "wss:" : "ws:"
Configure your WebSocket server with a valid TLS certificate

codeExamples.label: Use wss:// instead of ws://

title: WebSocket connection without origin validation
description: A WebSocket endpoint does not validate the Origin header, allowing connections from any website.
evidence: WebSocket endpoint without Origin header validation detected at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Without Origin validation, any website can open a WebSocket connection to your server using the victim's cookies — a cross-site WebSocket hijacking attack that gives attackers full access to any WebSocket API the user can reach.
explanation: WebSocket connections bypass CORS restrictions. The server must check the Origin header and reject connections from unexpected origins.
fixSteps: Check the Origin header on WebSocket upgrade requests
Reject connections from origins not in your allowlist
Use a CSRF token in the WebSocket connection URL or initial handshake message

codeExamples.label: Validate WebSocket origin server-side

title: postMessage receiver does not validate origin
description: A message event listener processes messages without checking event.origin, accepting messages from any window.
evidence: Message event handler without origin validation detected at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Without origin validation, any malicious page in the same browser can send postMessage events to your page and have them processed — potentially triggering privileged actions, passing tokens, or modifying application state.
explanation: Every postMessage receiver must validate event.origin against a trusted allowlist before processing the message.
fixSteps: Add an origin check: if (event.origin !== "https://trusted.example.com") return;
Use a Set of allowed origins for multiple trusted partners
Never use "*" as targetOrigin when sending sensitive data

codeExamples.label: Validate origin in postMessage handler

title: DOM XSS sinks with user-controlled input
description: User-controlled data is assigned to XSS-prone DOM sinks such as innerHTML, document.write, or eval without sanitization.
evidence: DOM XSS sink with user-controlled data detected at {{url}}.
riskImpact: DOM-based XSS allows attackers to inject and execute arbitrary JavaScript in the victim's browser — stealing session cookies, redirecting the user, making authenticated API requests on their behalf, or completely taking over their session.
explanation: DOM XSS occurs when user-controlled data flows into dangerous browser APIs without sanitization. Use safe DOM APIs and sanitize when HTML is required.
fixSteps: Replace innerHTML/outerHTML with textContent for plain text
Use DOMPurify.sanitize() when HTML rendering is required
Implement a Content Security Policy to limit script execution

codeExamples.label: Use textContent instead of innerHTML

title: Stack trace exposed in HTTP response
description: A server error response contains a full stack trace, revealing file paths, line numbers, framework versions, and application structure.
evidence: Stack trace exposed in server response at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Exposed stack traces reveal internal file paths, function names, dependency versions, and server architecture — information attackers use to identify known CVEs, craft targeted exploits, and understand application structure before attacking.
explanation: Stack traces should be logged server-side and never included in HTTP responses to clients.
fixSteps: Catch all errors and return generic messages to clients
Log full stack traces to a server-side logging system (Datadog, Sentry)
Set production error handling to never expose internal details

codeExamples.label: Suppress stack traces in production

title: Application stack trace visible in response
description: A stack trace from an unhandled exception is visible in the HTTP response body.
evidence: Stack trace in response body detected at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Stack traces expose technology stack, file paths, line numbers, and internal method names — reducing the effort required for an attacker to understand and target your application.
explanation: Handle all exceptions and return only generic error messages to clients in production.
fixSteps: Add a global error handler that logs errors and returns a safe response
Disable verbose error output in production configuration
Test that production error pages do not contain stack traces

codeExamples.label: Return generic error messages in production

title: SQL error message in page content
description: The page contains an SQL error message that reveals database type, query structure, or table names.
evidence: SQL error message detected in response body at {{url}}.
riskImpact: SQL error messages expose database type (MySQL, PostgreSQL, MSSQL), query structure, column names, and table names — giving attackers direct feedback for SQL injection probing without needing blind techniques.
explanation: Database errors must be caught server-side and never exposed to users.
fixSteps: Wrap all database calls in try/catch blocks
Return only generic error messages (status 500) to the client
Log the full SQL error server-side with a correlation ID for debugging

codeExamples.label: Catch database errors before they reach the response

title: PHP error/warning in page content
description: A PHP error, warning, or notice is displayed in the page output, revealing file paths and internal application details.
evidence: PHP error message detected in response at {{url}}.
riskImpact: PHP errors reveal absolute file paths (e.g., /var/www/html/app/...), PHP version, and internal application logic — helping attackers map the server filesystem and identify vulnerabilities.
explanation: PHP display_errors should be disabled in production; errors should be logged, not displayed.
fixSteps: Set display_errors = Off in php.ini or .htaccess
Set log_errors = On and log_errors to a server-side file
Set error_reporting to E_ALL in development only

codeExamples.label: Disable error display in PHP production config

title: ASP.NET error details in page content
description: ASP.NET error details including stack traces or YSOD (Yellow Screen of Death) are shown to end users.
evidence: ASP.NET error details detected in response at {{url}}.
riskImpact: ASP.NET YSOD pages expose .NET version, file paths, source code lines, and method signatures — information attackers use to identify patched vulnerabilities and target specific versions.
explanation: Enable custom error pages in production and disable detailed ASP.NET error output.
fixSteps: Set <customErrors mode="On"> in Web.config
Implement a global exception handler to log and return generic responses
Disable debug mode in production: <compilation debug="false">

codeExamples.label: Use custom errors in ASP.NET web.config
codeExamples.code:
<!-- web.config: suppress detailed errors in production -->
<configuration>
  <system.web>
    <customErrors mode="On" defaultRedirect="/error">
      <error statusCode="404" redirect="/not-found"/>
    </customErrors>
  </system.web>
</configuration>

title: Django debug mode enabled in production
description: Django's DEBUG = True is enabled in production, exposing detailed error pages with source code, local variables, and settings.
evidence: Django debug error page detected at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Django debug pages expose full source code at the error location, all local variables at every stack frame, all installed apps and middleware, and Django settings (including SECRET_KEY if it appears in a traceback) — a critical information disclosure.
explanation: DEBUG = True is appropriate only in development. Production deployments must have DEBUG = False.
fixSteps: Set DEBUG = False in production settings
Set ALLOWED_HOSTS to your actual domain names
Configure a proper error logging backend (Sentry, logging module)

codeExamples.label: Disable DEBUG mode in production
codeExamples.code:
# settings.py — never set DEBUG=True in production
DEBUG = os.environ.get('DJANGO_DEBUG', 'False') == 'True'

# ALLOWED_HOSTS must be set when DEBUG=False
ALLOWED_HOSTS = os.environ.get('ALLOWED_HOSTS', '').split(',')

title: Laravel debug mode enabled in production
description: Laravel's APP_DEBUG=true is enabled in production, showing detailed error pages with application code, stack traces, and environment variables.
evidence: Laravel Ignition debug page detected at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Laravel debug mode exposes full stack traces, local variables, all configuration values (including database credentials if they appear in traces), and can allow code execution via the Ignition debug interface on older versions.
explanation: APP_DEBUG must be false in production environments.
fixSteps: Set APP_DEBUG=false in .env for production
Set APP_ENV=production
Configure error logging to a server-side handler only

codeExamples.label: Set APP_DEBUG=false in production
codeExamples.code:
# .env (production)
