title: Sensitive data in Web Storage API
description: The page stores potentially sensitive data in localStorage or sessionStorage, which is accessible to all same-origin JavaScript.
evidence: Sensitive data stored in Web Storage API detected at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Data in localStorage and sessionStorage is accessible to any JavaScript running on the same origin — an XSS attack can exfiltrate all stored tokens, user data, and application state without the user's knowledge.
explanation: Web Storage should not contain authentication tokens, session identifiers, or PII. Use HttpOnly cookies for sensitive session state.
fixSteps: Move session tokens to HttpOnly cookies
Encrypt sensitive data before storing if Web Storage is unavoidable
Limit Web Storage to non-sensitive preferences only

codeExamples.label: Avoid storing sensitive data in localStorage

title: Geolocation API used without explicit user intent
description: The page accesses the Geolocation API. Ensure it is requested in response to a clear user action, not automatically on page load.
riskImpact: Automatically prompting for location permission on page load is poor UX and may violate GDPR/CCPA. Geolocation data collected without clear user consent or legitimate purpose is a privacy violation.
explanation: Geolocation should only be requested when the user initiates an action that clearly requires it.
fixSteps: Request geolocation only in response to an explicit user action (e.g., "Use my location" button)
Explain why location is needed before requesting permission
Do not store precise geolocation data without explicit consent

codeExamples.label: Request geolocation only when needed

title: Clipboard API accessed without user gesture
description: The page reads from or writes to the clipboard, which requires user permission and should only happen in response to user interaction.
riskImpact: Reading clipboard without user intent violates privacy — the clipboard may contain passwords, 2FA codes, or other sensitive data. Browsers require explicit user permission, but prompt fatigue may cause users to grant it without understanding the risk.
explanation: Clipboard read/write must be triggered by user gesture and clearly communicate why it needs clipboard access.
fixSteps: Only call navigator.clipboard.readText() in response to an explicit user gesture
Explain to users why clipboard access is needed
Prefer clipboard.write() over read() where possible

codeExamples.label: Request clipboard access with user gesture

title: Camera or microphone access requested
description: The page accesses the camera or microphone via getUserMedia. Ensure this is user-initiated and clearly communicated.
evidence: Camera/microphone access via getUserMedia detected at {{url}}.
riskImpact: If getUserMedia is called without clear user intent or context, users may not understand that recording has begun — a significant privacy violation. Malicious use could enable covert surveillance if combined with XSS.
explanation: Camera and microphone access must be requested in response to explicit user action with a clear UI indicator showing when recording is active.
fixSteps: Show a clear recording indicator when camera/mic is active
Stop tracks immediately when the user closes the feature
Only request access in response to an explicit user action

codeExamples.label: Request media access on explicit user action

title: HTML injection pattern detected
description: User-controlled input appears to be reflected in the HTML response without proper encoding, enabling HTML injection.
evidence: HTML injection pattern detected in response at {{url}}.
riskImpact: HTML injection allows attackers to insert arbitrary HTML content into the page — creating fake login forms (phishing), modifying page appearance, injecting links, or redirecting users — and may escalate to XSS if script tags can be injected.
explanation: All user-controlled values that appear in HTML output must be HTML-encoded to prevent injection.
fixSteps: HTML-encode all user-controlled values before inserting into HTML context
Use a templating engine that auto-escapes by default
Implement Content Security Policy to limit script execution

codeExamples.label: Escape user input before rendering

title: User input reflected in response without encoding
description: A query parameter or form field value is reflected back in the response body without HTML encoding, creating a reflected XSS or HTML injection vulnerability.
evidence: User input reflected unencoded in response at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Reflected user input allows an attacker to craft a URL that, when visited by a victim, injects arbitrary HTML or JavaScript into the page in the context of the victim's session — a reflected XSS attack.
explanation: All values from query strings, form fields, and headers must be HTML-encoded before being rendered in the response body.
fixSteps: HTML-encode all reflected values using your templating engine's auto-escape
Implement a Content Security Policy to block unauthorized scripts
Test with payloads like "><script>alert(1)</script> to verify encoding

codeExamples.label: Escape reflected values in server-side templates

title: Internal API version exposed in response
description: The API response includes an internal version string or build number that reveals implementation details.
evidence: API version or build number exposed in response at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Exposed API version strings allow attackers to look up known CVEs for that specific version and target unpatched vulnerabilities. Version enumeration is often the first step in a targeted attack.
explanation: API responses should not include internal version numbers or build identifiers.
fixSteps: Remove version strings from API response bodies
Remove X-Powered-By, X-Version, X-Build-Number headers
Use semantic versioning in URL paths (/api/v2/) without exposing internal build numbers

codeExamples.label: Remove API version from response headers

title: Server information disclosed in response
description: The HTTP response reveals server software name and/or version in the Server, X-Powered-By, or similar headers.
evidence: Server information disclosed in response headers at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Knowing the exact server software and version allows attackers to look up known vulnerabilities and CVEs for that specific version, reducing the effort needed to find an exploitable weakness.
explanation: Server identification headers should be removed or set to generic values.
fixSteps: Nginx: add "server_tokens off;" to your server block
Apache: set "ServerTokens Prod" and "ServerSignature Off"
Next.js: set poweredByHeader: false in next.config.mjs
Remove X-Powered-By, X-AspNet-Version, and X-Runtime headers

codeExamples.label: Suppress server version information
codeExamples.code:
# nginx.conf — hide server version
server_tokens off;

# Apache .htaccess
# ServerTokens Prod
# ServerSignature Off

title: Potential phishing lookalike domain detected in links
description: Outgoing links or redirects reference domains that closely resemble known trusted brands via typosquatting or homoglyph substitution.
evidence: Potential lookalike domain detected in links at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Links to phishing lookalike domains can redirect users to malicious sites that steal credentials or install malware — especially dangerous if these links appear in official pages and users assume they are legitimate.
explanation: Outbound links should be audited to ensure they do not reference typosquatted or lookalike domains.
fixSteps: Audit all outbound links for lookalike domains
Use rel="noopener noreferrer" on all external links
Consider monitoring your brand name for lookalike domain registrations

codeExamples.label: Register lookalike domains defensively

title: OpenGraph meta tag injection
description: User-controlled content is reflected in OpenGraph meta tags without proper HTML attribute encoding, enabling social graph manipulation.
evidence: User input reflected in OpenGraph meta tags at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Injecting values into OG meta tags lets attackers control the title, image, and description that appear when the URL is shared on social media — enabling convincing social engineering by making a link appear to show trusted content when shared.
explanation: OpenGraph meta tag values must be HTML-attribute-encoded when they include user-controlled content.
fixSteps: HTML-encode all user content in meta tag content attributes
Validate and sanitize dynamic OG tag values
Use Next.js metadata API which handles encoding automatically

codeExamples.label: Sanitize OpenGraph meta tag values

title: Service Worker with insecure fetch handler
description: The Service Worker intercepts all requests but does not validate origins or enforce HTTPS, potentially caching insecure responses.
evidence: Service Worker with insecure fetch handling detected at {{url}}.
riskImpact: An insecure Service Worker can cache and serve HTTP responses, downgrade HTTPS connections, and intercept all network traffic for the origin — extending the impact of any compromise beyond the page lifetime.
explanation: Service Workers should only intercept requests to known origins, enforce HTTPS, and not cache sensitive data.
fixSteps: Limit fetch interception to same-origin requests
Do not cache responses from HTTP origins
Avoid caching responses with authentication data (Authorization headers)

codeExamples.label: Register service worker only on HTTPS

title: Paste disabled on password field
description: The password input field has JavaScript that prevents pasting, making password manager use difficult.
evidence: Paste prevention detected on password field at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Blocking paste on password fields forces users to type passwords manually — discouraging the use of password managers and leading to shorter, weaker passwords that are easier to type. This increases the risk of account compromise.
explanation: NCSC and NIST recommend allowing paste on password fields to encourage password manager use.
fixSteps: Remove any onpaste="return false;" or paste event listeners from password fields
Allow paste on all password, email, and username fields
Consider allowing browsers to autofill credentials via autocomplete="current-password"

codeExamples.label: Remove paste prevention from password fields

title: Database connection string in source
description: A database connection string containing credentials is visible in client-accessible source or configuration files.
evidence: Database connection string with credentials detected at {{url}}.
riskImpact: An exposed database connection string gives an attacker direct access to your database — bypassing all application-level security controls and allowing them to read, modify, or delete all data.
explanation: Connection strings must be stored in environment variables or a secrets manager, never in source code or public-facing files.
fixSteps: Immediately rotate the database credentials
Move the connection string to an environment variable or secrets manager
Ensure .env files are in .gitignore and not served by the web server

codeExamples.label: Store connection strings in environment variables

title: SQL error message exposed in response
description: An SQL error message is returned in the HTTP response, revealing database type, query structure, or table names.
evidence: SQL error message exposed in response at {{url}}.
riskImpact: SQL error messages provide direct feedback to SQL injection probes — revealing database type, table names, column names, and query structure. This significantly reduces the effort required for a successful SQL injection attack.
explanation: All database errors must be caught and replaced with generic error messages before being sent to the client.
fixSteps: Add try/catch around all database calls
Return only generic error messages (500) to clients
Log detailed SQL errors to a server-side logging system only

codeExamples.label: Catch SQL errors before they reach the response

title: Sensitive source code comment in response
description: An HTML comment in the response contains potentially sensitive information such as file paths, credentials, or developer notes.
evidence: Sensitive HTML comment detected in response at {{url}}.
riskImpact: HTML comments are visible to anyone who views source, revealing internal paths, developer notes about security weaknesses, API endpoints, or occasionally credentials that were left during development.
explanation: Production HTML responses should not contain developer comments.
fixSteps: Remove all HTML comments from production templates
Use build-time comment stripping in your bundler
Audit HTML output for <!-- comments --> before deploying

codeExamples.label: Remove sensitive comments before deploying

title: Critical external script loaded without SRI
description: An externally hosted JavaScript file used on a critical page is loaded without a Subresource Integrity (SRI) hash.
evidence: Critical external script loaded without SRI at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Without SRI, if the CDN or third-party host is compromised, attackers can replace the script file with malicious code that runs in every visitor's browser — a supply chain attack with the full privileges of your page origin.
explanation: SRI hashes ensure that the file loaded matches the version you expected, blocking compromised or modified files.
fixSteps: Generate SRI hash: openssl dgst -sha384 -binary file.js | openssl base64 -A
Add integrity="sha384-HASH" and crossorigin="anonymous" to the <script> tag
Lock CDN URLs to specific versions to prevent unexpected changes

codeExamples.label: Add Subresource Integrity hash to CDN scripts
codeExamples.code:
<!-- Generate hash: openssl dgst -sha384 -binary file.js | openssl base64 -A -->
<script
  src="https://cdn.example.com/lib.js"
  integrity="sha384-<hash-here>"
  crossorigin="anonymous"
></script>

title: Hard-coded IP addresses in source
description: Private or public IP addresses are hard-coded in the client-side source, potentially revealing internal infrastructure.
evidence: Hard-coded IP address detected in JavaScript at {{url}}.
riskImpact: Hard-coded private IP addresses reveal internal network topology. Hard-coded public IPs reveal backend server locations that may be protected only by hostname-based firewall rules, and prevent easy infrastructure migration.
explanation: IP addresses should be read from configuration, not embedded in source code.
fixSteps: Replace hard-coded IPs with hostnames resolved from environment variables
Ensure private/internal IP addresses are never sent to client-side code
For internal service communication, use service discovery or environment variables

codeExamples.label: Move hardcoded IPs to environment variables
